Back: HyperText Transfer Protocol — the stateless application-layer protocol used for web communication

Front: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
Back: HTTPS = HTTP + TLS. TLS encrypts the connection, verifies server identity via certificates, and prevents tampering.

Front: What are the 5 HTTP request methods and their purpose?
Back: GET (read), POST (create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove)

Front: What HTTP status code means 'resource created successfully'?
Back: 201 Created

Front: What HTTP status code means 'valid auth but no permission'?
Back: 403 Forbidden (vs 401 = not authenticated at all)
A request is idempotent if making it multiple times has the same effect as making it once. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent. POST is not.
JSON Web Token — a compact, self-contained token with header.payload.signature. The payload carries claims (userId, role, exp). Signed with a secret key.
Each HTTP request must contain all info needed to process it. The server stores no session state between requests.
Domain Name System — the internet's phonebook. Translates human-readable domain names (example.com) to IP addresses (203.0.113.5).
1) Client sends SYN, 2) Server replies SYN-ACK, 3) Client sends ACK. This establishes a reliable connection before data transfer.

Front: What is the difference between TCP and UDP?
Back: TCP: reliable, ordered, connection-oriented (3-way handshake). UDP: fast, connectionless, no delivery guarantee. Use TCP for APIs, UDP for video streaming.

Front: What are HTTP headers used for?
Back: Pass metadata with requests/responses: auth tokens, content type, caching directives, CORS, cookies.

Front: What does CORS stand for and why does it exist?
Back: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. Browsers block JS from reading responses from a different domain by default. CORS headers allow controlled exceptions.
7-layer conceptual model: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. Each layer abstracts the one below it.

Front: What layer does HTTP operate on?
Back: Layer 7 — Application layer

Front: What is the difference between a web server and an app server?
Back: Web server serves static files and proxies requests (Nginx, Apache). App server runs application code and handles business logic (Node.js, Gunicorn).
Content Delivery Network — geographically distributed servers that cache static assets close to users, reducing latency.
A device or service that distributes incoming traffic across multiple server instances to prevent overload and enable scaling.

Front: What is the URL anatomy of https://api.example.com:443/users?page=2#top ?
Back: scheme=https, host=api.example.com, port=443, path=/users, query=page=2, fragment=top

Front: What does a 404 status code mean?
Back: Not Found — the requested resource does not exist on the server.
Saves a snapshot of the staged changes to the local repository history with a unique SHA hash.

Front: What is the difference between git fetch and git pull?
Back: fetch downloads remote changes without applying them. pull = fetch + merge (applies them to current branch).

Front: What is a git merge conflict?
Back: Occurs when two branches modify the same line differently. Git marks the conflict with <<<< ==== >>>> markers and requires manual resolution.
Rewrites commit history by replaying commits from one branch on top of another. Creates a linear history (vs merge which preserves the fork).
A file specifying patterns for files/directories Git should not track (node_modules, .env, build output).
Platform for packaging applications and dependencies into containers — lightweight, portable, isolated runtime environments.

Front: What is the difference between a Docker image and a container?
Back: Image = read-only blueprint (like a class). Container = running instance of an image (like an object).
Define and run multi-container applications from a YAML file. Manages networking, volumes, and service dependencies.
A NoSQL document database that stores data as BSON (binary JSON) documents in collections, instead of rows in tables.
An ODM (Object Document Mapper) library for Node.js that provides schema validation, middleware, and query helpers for MongoDB.
A JavaScript library for building component-based user interfaces. State changes trigger efficient DOM re-renders via a virtual DOM.
Functions prefixed with 'use' that let functional components use state and lifecycle features. e.g. useState, useEffect, useContext.
Runs side effects after render. The dependency array controls when it re-runs: [] = once, [x] = when x changes, omitted = every render.
A data-fetching library for React that manages server state: caching, background refresh, loading/error states, and mutation tracking.

Front: What is the difference between client state and server state?
Back: Client state: UI state (open menus, form input) — managed locally. Server state: data from APIs — managed by TanStack Query/SWR.
A utility-first CSS framework. Instead of writing CSS classes, you compose small utility classes (flex, p-4, text-xl) directly in HTML/JSX.
Representational State Transfer — an architectural style for APIs using HTTP verbs, resource-based URLs, and stateless communication.
Create, Read, Update, Delete — the four basic operations for persistent data. Map to POST, GET, PUT/PATCH, DELETE in REST.
A password hashing algorithm that applies a cost factor to deliberately slow down hashing, making brute-force attacks impractical.

Front: What is the principle of SOLID?
Back: 5 OOP design principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.
Test-Driven Development — write a failing test first, write minimal code to pass it, then refactor. Red-Green-Refactor cycle.

Front: What does npm run dev do in the webapp?
Back: Starts the Vite development server with Hot Module Replacement (HMR), allowing instant updates in the browser on file save.
A key-value pair set in the OS environment, used to configure applications without hardcoding secrets. Read via process.env.VAR.

Front: What is HTTPS and why is it mandatory?
Back: HTTP + TLS encryption. Mandatory because: protects data in transit, verifies server identity, required by browsers for PWAs and some APIs.
